text: Al-Būthī states that the term *al-maṣlaḥah* refers to every benefit sought by the Lawgiver (al-Shāri‘) for the benefit of human beings, namely the safeguarding of the five fundamental human goals of the religion, which are faith, life, mind, progeny and wealth, all based on a hierarchical system of necessity. He emphasizes that *maṣlaḥah* may serve as a supplementary principle in Islamic Shariah only when it observes the five regulatory conditions that he calls *ḍawābiṭ*, that is, the interest should fall under one of the five basic human goals, that it should not run counter to the contradiction of the Shariah through the words of the Ouran, or the sayings of the Prophet, or through analogical deductions, nor that it should take precedence of another interest. This paper aims at elucidating these issues while discussing al-Būthī’s refutation of al-Tūfī’s broader theories concerning *maṣlaḥah*. It endeavours to examine and to discuss also al-Būthī’s classification of interests within the boundaries of Shariah and to evaluate the ruling principles that he set up to restrict the roles of public interest in Islamic law.
headline: Fully Human Written
